primary_diagnosis.name: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (suspected inherited cardiomyopathy), symptomatic with exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmia and exertional presyncope

pathophysiology_summary: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy is an inherited (usually autosomal dominant, desmosomal) cardiomyopathy in which right ventricular myocardium is progressively replaced by fibrofatty tissue. That scar substrate creates re-entry circuits that generate ventricular ectopy and exercise-provoked ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation, and is a leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. Adrenergic drive during exertion lowers the arrhythmia threshold, which is why palpitations and (pre)syncope cluster around exercise. The clinical constellation here — exertional palpitations with near-syncope, an arrhythmic (not vasovagal) prodrome, frequent ventricular ectopy, right-sided signs (parasternal RV heave, mildly elevated JVP), and a family history of premature sudden death — is the classic ARVC red-flag picture. Definitive diagnosis rests on 12-lead ECG (e.g. epsilon waves, T-wave inversion V1–V3), Holter (ventricular arrhythmia burden), and cardiac MRI showing RV dilatation/dysfunction and fibrofatty replacement — none of which are obtainable in a telehealth chat. The immediate danger is a lethal ventricular tachyarrhythmia; exercise restriction and urgent specialist evaluation are the priorities pending diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmia due to inherited cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional palpitations with near-syncope in a young athlete is a cardiac red-flag pattern until proven otherwise.
Common cause of young-adult near-syncope, but exertional onset with a palpitation-first prodrome is atypical and should not anchor the assessment.
Patient is anxious, but anxiety does not explain an exertionally-triggered arrhythmic prodrome or the family history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should already be treating exertional (pre)syncope as potentially cardiac and NOT reflexively reassuring.

differential.diagnosis: Inherited cardiomyopathy / channelopathy with exertional ventricular arrhythmia (ARVC leading)
differential.evidence_basis: Palpitation-first arrhythmic prodrome, exertional trigger, worsening over two months, plus a first-degree-adjacent family history of premature sudden death.
Also causes exertional syncope and SCD in the young; kept in view but no ejection systolic murmur and the signs are right-sided.

differential.diagnosis: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia / other channelopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Exercise-triggered arrhythmic syncope with structurally 'near-normal' resting appearance is the differential group; discriminated only by ECG/MRI/genetics.
Downgraded once the arrhythmic prodrome and family history emerge — exertional syncope is a vasovagal red flag.
Cannot account for the exertional arrhythmic trigger, the right-sided signs, or the family history of sudden death.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Discriminators are the exertional trigger, the palpitation-first prodrome, and the family history of premature SCD. Anchoring on vasovagal or anxiety here is the key reasoning error.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected ARVC (inherited cardiomyopathy) with exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmia — pending ECG/Holter/cardiac MRI
differential.evidence_basis: Full red-flag constellation; a telehealth service cannot confirm it but must recognise the danger and route to urgent in-person cardiac assessment.
Remains on the do-not-miss list until echocardiography/MRI clarifies the structural substrate.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct telehealth end-state is 'time-critical inherited-cardiomyopathy pattern, cannot confirm remotely, urgent in-person cardiac assessment plus strict exercise cessation and recurrence safety-netting' — not a confident diagnosis and not reassurance.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope during or immediately after exertion is a cardinal red flag for a life-threatening ventricular arrhythmia or structural/inherited heart disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling as a simple faint or anxiety and clearing the patient to continue exercise risks sudden cardiac death.

red_flags.description: Family history of premature sudden unexplained cardiac death (uncle, ~age 30)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Premature sudden death in a relative sharply raises the pre-test probability of an inherited cardiomyopathy or channelopathy and mandates cardiac work-up plus family screening.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to ask about or weight the family history is the single most common way this diagnosis is missed.

red_flags.description: Progressive worsening of exertional arrhythmic symptoms over two months
red_flags.clinical_significance: An escalating pattern signals an evolving arrhythmic substrate and increasing risk — it removes any 'one-off benign event' reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage as an isolated faint misses a deteriorating high-risk trajectory.

red_flags.description: Right ventricular signs on examination (parasternal RV heave, mildly elevated JVP) with frequent ventricular ectopy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective right-heart signs support a structural RV cardiomyopathy — but these are clinician-examination findings and cannot be elicited in telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to the AI Doctor directly (unobtainable remotely); reinforces why in-person assessment is mandatory rather than remote reassurance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling exertional near-syncope in a young, anxious person as a simple vasovagal faint. Exertional onset and a palpitation-first prodrome argue strongly against reflex syncope.
Attributing palpitations and presyncope to anxiety because the patient is visibly distressed — anxiety cannot explain an exercise-triggered arrhythmic event or the family history.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from normal resting observations
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured by normal resting HR, BP and SpO2. Inherited cardiomyopathies are intermittently arrhythmic; a normal resting snapshot never excludes a dangerous exertional arrhythmia.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to weight the family history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not asking about, or not acting on, premature sudden death in a relative — the key epidemiological red flag that shifts this from benign faint to inherited-cardiomyopathy work-up.

Attempting to diagnose or clear this remotely. The confirming tests (ECG, Holter, cardiac MRI) and examination are unavailable in chat, so the only safe action is to recognise the pattern and route to urgent in-person cardiac assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG features (epsilon waves, T-wave inversion V1–V3, ventricular ectopy morphology)
Holter / ambulatory monitor arrhythmia burden and morphology
Cardiac MRI showing RV dilatation, dysfunction and fibrofatty replacement
Echocardiographic structural assessment (to weigh HCM vs ARVC)
Auscultation for murmurs and palpation for a parasternal RV heave
Objective rhythm at the time of symptoms
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a time-critical inherited-cardiomyopathy / exertional-ventricular-arrhythmia pattern; direct urgent same-day in-person cardiac assessment (ED or urgent cardiology with same-day ECG); instruct immediate and complete cessation of all exertion/sport; safety-net that any recurrence at rest, actual syncope, or chest pain means call 000 immediately.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific label (ARVC vs HCM vs channelopathy) cannot and should not be fixed remotely — over-committing to one is a pitfall. The dominant risk, however, is UNDER-action: reassuring or deferring a young athlete with exertional syncope and a family history of sudden death.

guidelines_referenced.reference: 2015 International Task Force / HRS Consensus — ARVC diagnostic criteria
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: ARVC diagnosis integrates ECG (epsilon waves, repolarisation/depolarisation abnormalities), arrhythmia, imaging (RV dysfunction/fibrofatty replacement), and family history/genetics — none obtainable in telehealth, mandating in-person work-up.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC 2022 ventricular arrhythmia / SCD prevention guidelines
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Exertional syncope with a family history of premature sudden death warrants urgent specialist cardiac evaluation and restriction from competitive/strenuous exercise pending assessment.
